title: Why Modern Text User Interfaces Are an Accessibility Nightmare
summary: A new article reveals that modern Text User Interfaces (TUIs) like Claude Code's Ink-based renderer often use complex terminal escape codes and layered rendering, which can break screen readers and accessibility tools. Developers often assume terminal applications are inherently accessible, but this misconception may exclude users with disabilities from critical developer tools and workflows, counteracting ongoing efforts toward inclusive software. Issues include ANSI escape sequences for complex layouts, overlapping curse repositioning that confuses screen readers, and settings like CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1 to reduce visual noise, all of which hinder sequential text parsing.
context: Text User Interfaces (TUIs) are full-screen applications inside terminal emulators, using character grid, box-drawing, and colors to create interactive forms and panes. Unlike streaming command-line output, they actively manage the entire screen, often redrawing portions. This conflicts with traditional screen readers designed for linear text streams. Modern frameworks like Ink render components into escape codes, which can produce flickering and inaccessible layering.
discussion: Community reaction is largely critical of modern TUI design, calling it bloated and misguided. Some users prefer the original terminal streaming model, while others doubt that developers actually believe TUIs are universally accessible. A few speculate about sprite-based or alternative TUI paradigms.
